item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I know that this article is old and that the original image had to be removed in November last year for be non-compliant with the required license on the site, besides I don't know if the author is currently active. However, because he mentions in the article that SCP-1280 looks like the worm Necator americanus, I found some pictures that could serve as a replacement (these images should comply with the required license)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Necator_Americanus_L3_x1000_12-2007.jpg photo of N. americanus

https://ccsearch.creativecommons.org/photos/37dfd7c1-837c-4906-9e13-b7ae735b0ded egg of N. americanus

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Necator_americanus_(YPM_IZ_095227).jpeg photo 2 of N. americanus (looks as a little monster)

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Necator_americanus_(YPM_IZ_095221).jpeg photo 3 of N. americanus (this one also looks as a little monster)

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Necator_americanus_(YPM_IZ_095225).jpeg photo 4 of N. americanus

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image is credited to one David Scharf of the Science Photo Library, with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As sound as the scientific parts are, they just went on too long, and I kept thinking, "get on with it, what does it do?" Then the really cool part ended as soon as it began. I wish it had been the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a well written biological scip that actually passes scrutiny on the science sections so of course it's a +1. That said: This here? This is how you get Aboleths!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this but I have to ask. Does the sequence in the footnote mean something? Like it's supposed to be a signature or similar from whoever made it.

I know this was made years ago so this probably won't be answered but I can't imagine the point of showing it if it doesn't have any significance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say, this is a perfect SCP. It's scientific, it builds off of something ordinary, and the end result is split down the middle between strangely sentimental and terrifying. Reminds me a lot of 1457 but more technical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awsome article with a creepy picture and terryfing anomalous effects. Nice job.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 104 now. Excellent article, love all the implications and effects. I almost wish I'd thought of this one myself, but I'd hate to deny you the honor!
…well, +102 now, but no one else seems to have noted this hitting triple digits. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just really like the name of the MTF. "Maz Hatters." That's just awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture is terrifying but the article itself is just fascinating. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good one: an interesting effect with some plausible technobabble to back it up, with some creepy implications at the end that aren't fully spelled out but left to develop in the mind of the reader. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I read the title I wanted to upvote just for that amazing pun. I read the article, it's bloody awesome. I really like the injecting of memory that isn't yours, something i've always found creepy. Only issue I have is the picture, it looks kind of stupid. Might I suggest this instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is a little difficult to get through at first, but besides that this is really good. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let me just say that I appreciate the level of biological detail included in your description. Personally, I always feel as though such details add that oh-so-delicious blend of professionalism and disinterest. While it may not be the most inventive SCP, credit goes to the amount of effort I detect in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All this up here ^^.
Plus, really excellent techno-babble. Just enough "heisenberg compensator" in the chemical mechanism to make it realistic and avoid any obvious violations of known science. Kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I'd just been having an argument with someone over whether or not it was possible to make a new, interesting parasite SCP. They said it couldn't be done. I think you've proved them wrong. Good show, Murphy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I was like "Oh, someone took the parasite-worms-that-make-you-great from Futurama and made it only affect the brain". Even though I was sure it was a coincidence.

And then I started reading things about sharing memories.

And then I read about it likely being first released (that we know of) via crop duster, meaning that there was either a main infection in a DISTRIBUTION HUB or, as Voct posited, someone is intentionally spreading the stuff. Ye gods.

Also, that picture will be in my nightmares tonight.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good picture, great idea. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To the extent that I could only understand about half of it, I bought it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got a lot of advice and assistance on the genetics bits from InitHello.

This was, in part, an experiment to see how plausible i could make an article with a lot of technobabble, so I ended up doing a decent amount of research.

item.title: Re: Origins
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if the reason was to create human hive-societies… or if this was a botched attempt at recording human memory for later retrieval?

Because infecting a species with a parasite that stores their memories seems like a handy way to study them from afar, particularly if you can get a lot of memories from the group as a whole out of one individual.

item.title: Origins
item.content:encoded:
Aerosolized distribution?

Someone made this. Deliberately. And then released it on human populations. For… a reason.

That is terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As scientific as this article is (on the high end of the spectrum; I mean, DNA sequences, really?), it's overall very a simple object and very effective. I'm rather impressed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… This sounds odd but I wouldn't mind terribly if I had this cute little bugger in me. My recall is terrible.

+1 for being a well thought out and well written article. Also communist worms ftw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh I liked the biological mechanism you conjured up to explain the organisms workings. But reading this definitely makes me upvote it:
"…The exchange of memories in this manner can lead to the erosion of personal identity as members lose track of which events they experienced directly and which were remembered second-hand. In one extremely advanced infestation, members of an entire village referred to themselves by a single given name and displayed behaviors seen previously only in colonies of eusocial insects."
Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good, but oh my god is it long. Try to cut back, i guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. Not a super killy memetic disease but rather, a far more horrifying effect when pondered slightly.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this thing's effects, but that's not why I'm upvoting. I'm upvoting because I'm terrified of parasitic worms. The idea that something in my body could improve my memory but destroy who I am is more than enough to secure my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As strange as this sounds; I suddenly cannot find what I was talking about. I think it has something to do with the fact that I just woke up when I wrote that. Upvote for the SCP and an apology for possible confusion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Murphy, I'd explain a bit more as to what constitutes "a single visual memory"—how long is the memory, what exactly is remembered. Some examples would help. Also, in the containment procedures you say something like "If a sample is found, a course of soandso is to be applied…" but it's not clear what that's being applied to. Other than that I love the medical jargon and SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. A comparatively common creature with a unique, potentially horrifying mutation. You get something good (improved memory) with a scary side effect (loss of self). +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any specific suggestions on tone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other than some tonal issues (preventing my upvote) I like it. With some cleaning up, I think it could be functional.
